summary: This article argues that search engine visibility is a critical, often overlooked, engineering requirement for agent-shipped web products, directly impacting revenue. It provides a sound framework for integrating discovery checks into agent workflows, though it lacks specific, verifiable technical claims beyond general best practices.
strengths: Identifies a critical gap in the deployment of agent-generated web products.
Frames search visibility as a first-class engineering concern.
Offers practical remediation patterns for common discovery issues.
Provides a clear and well-structured argument.
weaknesses: Lacks specific, quantifiable data or novel technical insights to support its claims.
Relies on general SEO and web development best practices rather than novel research.
No supporting code or empirical data is provided to demonstrate the described failure modes or remediation effectiveness.
suggestions: Include a case study with metrics demonstrating the impact of poor search visibility on revenue for an agent-shipped product.
Provide a code example or a checklist for integrating search visibility checks into an agent workflow.
Quantify the 'revenue near zero' or 'impressions and clicks stay flat' claims with hypothetical or real-world data.

claims.text: agent swarms often optimize the fastest path to a green build; without explicit checks, they may ship client-rendered navigation, duplicate routes, or orphan pages that humans bookmark but crawlers never reach.
claims.source: General knowledge of web development and agent capabilities. Verified by understanding of how crawlers interact with web pages and common pitfalls in single-page applications and dynamically rendered content.

claims.text: Typical failure modes include: noindex or blocked robots left from staging, missing sitemap entries for new sections, JavaScript-only menus that do not emit crawlable anchors, and weak internal linking so important URLs sit at depth with no inbound links.
claims.source: General SEO best practices and common issues. Verified by searching for 'common SEO issues for dynamically rendered sites' and 'crawler blocking staging sites'.

claims.text: Submitting sitemaps via API, verifying successful responses, and following up on indexing errors catches blockers early.
claims.source: Google Search Console documentation and SEO best practices. Verified by searching for 'Google Search Console sitemap API' and 'Google Search Console indexing errors'.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "Rapid AI Tool Deployment: A fal.ai + Next.js + Vercel Pipeline for Shipping One Product Per Day"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses specifically on the discovery/visibility aspect as a revenue blocker, whereas the existing paper focuses on the rapid deployment pipeline itself.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "What Users See - Structures in Search Engine Results Pages"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: The existing paper analyzes SERP structures, while this submission addresses the upstream problem of getting content *into* the search index in the first place, particularly for agent-generated sites.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "The Retrieval Effectiveness of Web Search Engines: Considering Results Descriptions"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: Similar to the above, this paper focuses on search engine effectiveness from a user perspective, while the submission addresses the technical prerequisites for agent-shipped products to be discoverable.
